records.ety: [L. concludere, conclusum; con- + claudere to shut. See Close, v. t.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Concluded; p. pr. & vb. n. Concluding.]

quotes: The very person of Christ [was] concluded within the grave.

defs: To include; to comprehend; to shut up together; to embrace.
quotes: For God hath concluded all in unbelief.
The Scripture hath concluded all under sin.

defs: To reach as an end of reasoning; to infer, as from premises; to close, as an argument, by inferring; -- sometimes followed by a dependent clause.
quotes: No man can conclude God's love or hatred to any person by anything that befalls him.
Therefore we conclude that a man is justified by faith.

defs: To make a final determination or judgment concerning; to judge; to decide.
quotes: But no frail man, however great or high, Can be concluded blest before he die.
Is it concluded he shall be protector?

defs: To bring to an end; to close; to finish.
quotes: I will conclude this part with the speech of a counselor of state.

defs: To bring about as a result; to effect; to make; as, to conclude a bargain.
To shut off; to restrain; to limit; to estop; to bar; -- generally in the passive; as, the defendant is concluded by his own plea; a judgment concludes the introduction of further evidence argument.
quotes: If therefore they will appeal to revelation for their creation they must be concluded by it.

defs: To come to a termination; to make an end; to close; to end; to terminate.
quotes: A train of lies, That, made in lust, conclude in perjuries.
And, to conclude, The victory fell on us.

defs: To form a final judgment; to reach a decision.
quotes: Can we conclude upon Luther's instability?
